data.title: The Curious Case Of The Term Premium
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome The Macro Investigator as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

An unusual bear steepening event has disrupted Treasury market expectations, defying historical rate-cutting cycles. With inflation dynamics shifting and policy uncertainty rising, investors face three potential paths forward. Which one is most likely, and how should investors prepare?

Scenario 1: Slowdown in economy, and a flat or inverted yield curve

High money market rates like the environment we are in now are indicative of the late business cycle stages, prompting investors to anticipate a slowdown. In this scenario, investors could increase treasury and investment-grade bond holdings, benefiting from Fed rate cuts during potential recessions.

Scenario 2: Short-term rates decrease, leading to a bull steepening

The second scenario anticipates a bull steepening due to falling short-term rates as inflation improves and the labor market softens. The Fed may cut rates further, leading to a steeper yield curve. Investors might favor short-dated treasuries or short/medium term corporate bonds at this moment while monitoring money market rates, and later investing in the longer end of the curve when it stabilizes.

Scenario 3: Shift towards a higher interest rate environment

The third scenario envisions a higher interest rate environment, with an upward shift in the yield curve reflecting this change. Investors may anticipate the 10-year yield surpassing 5%.

Introduction

The bond market, in particular, the US treasuries, has been showing interesting changes in recent weeks, where the yield on 10-year treasuries has increased by more than 100bps since the September lows, while the Fed has been lowering its policy rate. This is highly unusual, as during the previous seven cutting cycles by the Fed going back to the 1980s, the yield on the 10-year Treasury was lower 100% of the time 100 days after the first rate cut.

However, this was not the case this time round, as we have seen that the 10-year yield has, in fact, gone up, leading to the phenomenon coined by economists as "bear steepening" This is a rather rare condition, as there have been only about 15 occurrences of bear steepenings in the US since 1960, and like the inverted yield curve, has been a warning bell that screams looming recession.

However, the yield curve has been uninverted since early August 2024, and there's still almost no signs of any recession. Also, since the recent high in mid-January, the 10-year yield has been gradually decreasing. With no apparent recession in sight even with these two recession indicators showing, it really begs the question on many investors' mind, is this time truly different?

Chart 1: Change in 10-year yield post first Fed cut, Source: JPM

Treasury yield curve and the Fed

To start, let's first look at how the changes in Fed funds rate affect the yield curve. The Fed funds rate represents the rate banks charge each other for overnight loans, and as such, it most directly affects the shortest-term Treasuries. Thus, when the Fed cuts its policy rate, the most immediate impact would be on the shortest end of the yield curve.

That being said, the longer end of the yield curve may not reflect the Fed cuts as fast as the short end, or in recent case, have even increased for a period of time. The reason for this discrepancy is because while the short end of the yield curve is more sensitive to the Fed Funds rate, the long end more broadly reflects investors' long-term expectations for economic performance. To understand this, let's take a step back and breakdown the building blocks of treasury yields. Generally speaking, the yield of treasuries consists of three components: The short end risk-free return, the term premium, and the recession premium.

1. Short end risk-free rate (Money market rates)

The short end risk-free rate, or money market rates represents the compensation that investors receive for lending money to the government over a short tenor, which we can proxy as the three month T-bill (with yields largely similar to the Fed funds rate). The Fed has a strong impact on the short-term rates and will usually increase or decrease simultaneously as the fed funds rate.

2. Term premium

Next, as longer-term treasuries generally demand a higher yield to compensate for future uncertainties, the term premium is the difference between long-term yields and short-term yields (Conventionally taken as the spread between 10-year and 2-year treasury yields). There have been many debates on what impacts the term premium, but broadly speaking, the term premium can be affected by the following:

I. Better than expected growth in the economy: As the overall economy is in better shape than initially expected, this would decrease the number of cuts expected from the central bank. A robust economy will therefore reflect higher yields in the long end of the curve.

II. Higher inflation expectations: On a related note, if the expectation is for higher, or sticky inflation, the long-term curve is likely to reflect a higher yield. Trump's policies are also arguably inflationary and are likely more pro-growth, which leads to a more benign environment for businesses and deal makings. All this could lead to a more robust economy with higher prices. Investors, in anticipation of this scenario, may have priced in a higher yield on the long-term rates.

III. Government Fiscal deficit: In fiscal year 2024, the US federal deficit was $1.833 trillion. This was the third-largest deficit in US history. The debt to GDP ratio is at 120%, the highest in modern history, outside of the covid era. Moreover, the high-interest rate environment has led to the interest on the federal debt to exceed $1 trillion for the first time in 2024.

The mounting debt has definitely been a growing concern for many investors, and as such, investors would command a higher yield for them to invest in treasuries over concern of the deficit.

Chart 2: US debt to GDP %, Source: Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED)

IV. Treasury auction supply and demand: Just like any other market, the treasuries market is also largely affected by the supply and demand dynamics. U.S. debt is issued through auctions held by the U.S. Treasury. One key metric to look at for treasury auctions is the bid to cover ratio.

The bid-to-cover ratio is the dollar amount of bids received in a Treasury security auction versus the amount sold. Generally, a bid to cover ratio of above 2 is considered healthy, indicating strong demand for government bonds.

The bid to cover ratio is currently at 2.53 right now, although a slight drop from the previous reading of 2.7, but still healthy. As seen from the chart below, where the orange line is the 10-year treasury bid to cover ratio, and the purple line is the 10-year yield, when the bid to cover ratio increases, the yield tends to fall, reflecting the higher demand, vice versa. The ratio has dropped slightly from the previous reading, reflecting the slight weakness of the demand for treasuries, which could be due to the above reasons.

Chart 3: 10 Yr Treasury bid-to-cover ratio Vs 10 Yr Treasury yield, Source: Thomson Reuters

3. Recession Premium

By all conventional standards, the US treasuries are one of the safest hedges against adverse economic outcomes and recessions. As such, when the market has the sense of an imminent recession, investors tend to shift their money to longer-term treasuries by selling their shorter-term treasuries in anticipation of the future. The result - An inverted yield curve, which is traditionally seen as a recession signal. We can think of the fall in yield due to the rise in demand for treasuries during a recession as the "recession premium" - the yield that you have to pay for protection against recession.

Putting it all together

From the building blocks of the treasury yields, we can see that the first two components, the money market rate, and the term premium are both additive to the yield of treasuries, whereas the recession premium would be subtracted from the overall yield. That being said, a higher money market rate will generally be followed by a low term premium. Think of the high money market rate as a high starting point, where there is limited room for the long-end yield to increase, given the already high-interest rates. The term premium is also typically inversely related to the level of the Fed Funds rate - The yield curve generally demonstrates a high term premium when Fed funds rate is low, and a low term premium when Fed funds rate is high. At high levels of Fed funds rate, there should be a lower term premium, vice versa, showing the inverse relationship between Fed funds rate and the term premium.

Chart 4: Fed funds rate Vs Term Premium, Source: FRED

With low rates in the money market, the expectation is that future economic growth and inflation is likely to increase, thereby increasing the term premium. The recession premium would also be very low, given the benign economic environment. The result - An upward sloping yield curve, with low money market rates and high long-term yield.

Conversely, at high level of money market rates, the expectation is that the tight monetary conditions would lead to slower economic growth and lower inflation. It is more likely that yields would fall rather than increase, moving forward, given the already high rates, thus a fall in term premium is likely. Also, there is usually concern of an upcoming recession at higher levels of money market rates, as it is typically during the late stage of the business cycle where money market rates are high - Central bank has elevated rates to slow down economic growth and inflation.

The higher money market rates, lower term premium, and a higher recession premium will therefore lead to higher rates in the short end, and lower rates in the long end, leading to a flat or inverted yield curve. That is why it is very rare to see a bear steepener at an elevated money market rate, with rising term premium, like the one we have seen in mid-January.

What does this mean for investors?

Given what we have discussed that high levels of money market rates should be accompanied by a low term premium, vice versa, and what we have seen in the yield curve is an anomaly - High money market rates with high term premium, one of three scenarios is likely to happen. Depending on your personal view, the following ideas could be taken into consideration in relation with the given scenarios.

Scenario 1: Recession premium rises, leading to a flat or even inverted yield curve

As mentioned previously, high levels of money market rates, like those now, are generally present at the late stage of the business cycle, where economic activity is at its peak. Thus, it is only natural for investors to expect a slowdown from here. As such, the practical course of action would be to stock up on long duration treasuries in anticipation of the economic slowdown (10 year treasuries are yielding 4.535% and 30 year treasuries are yielding 4.749% at the time of writing, which represents a good entry point given its high yield). Also, if a recession does come around (which is the default view of this scenario), the Fed is likely to cut rates, benefiting long duration fixed income instruments.

Apart from long duration treasuries, investors who think that this scenario is likely to play out can also increase their holdings in investment grade bonds as well, given their relative resistance to economic stress. As an example, the S&P 500 Investment grade Corporate Bond Index has a yield to worst of 5.25%, with a weighted average maturity of 10.41 years. Treasuries and higher duration credit are likely to do well as the Fed cuts rate.

That being said, there are minimal noticeable stress in the economic environment right now, which sees a recession scenario as unlikely. However, given the uncertain macro environment going forward, it may be prudent to add some exposures to treasuries and investment grade bonds as a hedge against risks.

Scenario 2: Short-term rates decrease, leading to a bull steepening

The second scenario would be that the short-term rates should decrease as well, leading to the counterpart of a bear steepening, which is a bull steepening scenario - A lower overall rates, with the short end rates falling faster than the long end rates. The argument for this scenario is that with the recent progress in inflation, and a still healthy, but softening labor market, the fed is likely to continue its rate cut going forward, thereby reducing the short-term rates. This would lead to the steepening of the yield curve, with lower money market rates and higher long-term rates.

Although the term premium has increased in recent weeks, and peaked in mid-January, do keep in mind that we have just exited from a yield curve inversion not too long ago. Even though the term premium may seem high now, it is still lower than the median range of 81bps, with data dating back to the 1970s. The term premium right now is about 20bps and peaked around 41bps in mid-January. If we take the median projected fed funds target at year-end of 3.875%, this would lead to a year-end target of the 10-year yield at around 4.675%, using the historical medium term premium.

If investors expects this scenario to play out, they can consider a barbell strategy, investing in short-dated treasuries (2 year yielding 4.292% at the time of writing), or short/medium-dated corporate bonds, at this moment, given that the short end is expected to decrease, and adding on to duration when long end yield starts to stabilize. Investors can consider adding to short-dated high-yield corporate bonds for the additional yield return at the short end of the curve, given their lower duration and lower interest rate risk. As an example, iShares Short Duration High Yield Corporate Bond UCITS ETF has a YTM of 6.63% and an effective duration of 2.10 years. Alternatively, they can choose to park their funds at the higher level of money market rates first while waiting for the yield curve to stabilize before making their decision. Investors could also invest in the medium part of the curve now if they are concerned about the interest rate risk. An example would be the PIMCO Income Fund, which is mostly investment grade bonds, yielding 7.26% and effective duration of 4.56 years.

This scenario seems more likely to play out in the long term, given the deviation from the historical median term premium right now, and also the further rate cuts going forward.

Chart 5: US Treasury Term premium (10 yr treasury yield - 2 yr treasury yield), Source: FRED

Scenario 3: Shift towards a higher interest rate environment

The third scenario is that we are shifting towards a higher interest rate environment, and the yield curve as a whole should be moving upwards, to reflect higher interest rates at each point of the curve. The argument for this scenario would be that we are currently entering into an overall high rates environment. Although we are currently in the highest rate environment post financial crisis, we have indeed seen higher interest rates before, notably in the 1980s, where Fed funds rate was around 20%, and 10 year treasury yield was around 15.82% at its highest in 1981.

However, a few things were different compared to back then. For one, inflation was the highest on record, at around 14%. Also, debt to GDP was less than 50% for most of the 1980s. This meant that the central bank had more capacity to raise rates back then, without putting too much stress on the national debt. The US is currently at 120% debt to GDP right now. As such, every percentage increase in the treasury rates would lead to substantial strain on the debt level.

Therefore, if we look at the debt level, it seems unlikely that interest rates would stay higher for too long. Finally, the Fed's path forward still looks for further rate cuts, albeit at a slower pace. That being said, if this scenario indeed plays out, where higher rates are the new norm, 10-year treasuries could easily break 5% (If we take the 2-year yield at the time of writing, which is around 4.2% and add on the median 81bps term premium, we would have a 10-year yield of 5%)

Investors in this camp could expect the 10-year yield to increase further before investing their capital to the longer end of the curve as the long-term yield is expected to increase further (with 5% as the psychological level to watch out for) Also, if yield levels indeed increase, investors could expect equities to underperform, and as such could consider investing in long-term investment grade bonds as a portfolio diversifier. An example would be the iShares 10+ Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF.

Chart 6: Fed Funds rate Vs 10 Yr Treasury yields, Source: FRED

Conclusion and final thoughts

The bear steepener in the yield curve has definitely been a rare event, an outlier in the valley of the norm. That said, rare events do happen, and they will pass on with time. However, what we can do as investors is to make educated guesses on what could happen, and to prepare for the anticipated event that, we think, is the most plausible.

Given the above three scenarios, we are starting to see scenario one playing out, with an overall lower yield levels and long-term yields falling faster than short-term yield - A bull flattening scenario. This could be due to investors moving towards longer-term bonds amidst the uncertain economic backdrop these few weeks.

However, in the long term, scenario 2 is likely to stand the test of time, as uncertainty dissipates and the term premium returns to the median level. A good strategy now could be to lock in the high yield at the short and medium part of the curve, while waiting for the 10 year to reach the 4.6% level before investing in the long end of the curve.
